preamble: The change from the glottal fricative /h/ to the palatal glide /j/ is a process of fortition or 'strengthening' where a sound with only laryngeal articulation gains a specific oral articulation. This often happens before a high front vowel like /i/, where the /h/ assimilates the palatal quality of the vowel, effectively becoming /j/.

examples.note: The palatal allophone of /h/ before front vowels, [ç], can merge with /j/.
/h/ is often realized as a palatal fricative or glide in this context.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
